profile.behavior: An almost continuously aerial insectivore; flies rapidly with stiff, shallow wingbeats and clings rather than perches when at rest.
field_marks: Very long, narrow, strongly curved wings
Short, tapered tail with stiff, bristle-like tips
Dark gray-brown overall, with a slightly paler throat
Tiny feet; clings to vertical surfaces rather than perching
Very short, broad-based bill that is often hard to see
profile.habitat: Open airspace over a wide variety of terrain where flying insects are available; nests and roosts on enclosed vertical surfaces, including chimneys, cavities, caves, and similar structures.

sources.title: Chimney Swift Identification — All About Birds

findings: Identity is correct: the main bird shows the species’ dark gray-brown plumage, slightly paler throat, tiny clinging feet, short broad-based bill, long narrow folded wings, and vertical-surface posture; the reference photos support these traits.
The plate’s two auxiliary studies (wing and head) are labeled illustrations rather than additional whole birds; the primary bird has one head, one beak, two wings, two legs, and one tail.
All visible taxonomy, behavior, habitat, and field-mark claims agree with Cornell’s live identification page. Length and weight also agree.

The printed wingspan (10.6–11.8 in / 27–30 cm) agrees with Cornell but differs from Audubon’s live page, which gives 11–12.6 in / 27–32 cm. This source discrepancy should be resolved or cited before treating the plate as a single-source measurement reference.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears in the candidate.
The illustrated eye is somewhat enlarged/stylized and the folded-wing/tail silhouette is more elongated than the clinging reference photo, but neither creates a material species-identification error.

verification_sources.title: Chimney Swift Identification — All About Birds
